components.title: Why Shelter Matters
components.content: Explore the relationship between shelter design and the land. Log cabin notch geometry reflects the available timber. The wigwam dome reflects the need for rapid construction. Both are responses to the same question: how do we stay warm and dry? Examine the physical constraints that shape shelter across traditions.

components.title: Notch Geometry Basics
components.content: Practice drawing the saddle notch and dovetail notch profiles. Understand why the saddle notch is self-draining (water runs out) and why the dovetail notch is self-locking (compression tightens the joint). Sketch both and label the bearing surfaces.

components.title: The Land Shapes the House
components.content: Follow the progression from temporary camp shelters to permanent structures. Understand how material availability (hardwood vs. cedar vs. birch) shapes construction technique. The Haudenosaunee longhouse, the Anishinaabe wiigiwaam, and the Appalachian cabin are all correct answers to the same question.

components.title: Frame Geometry
components.content: Calculate the rafter angles for a basic gable roof at 6/12 pitch and 9/12 pitch. Understand why pitch affects snow load (steeper sheds snow), drainage, and interior headroom. Demonstrate the relationship between span, rise, and run using the 3-4-5 right triangle.

components.title: Dome vs. Rectangle
components.content: Compare the dome shelter (wigwam, igloo, yurt) to the rectangular shelter (cabin, longhouse). Surface-to-volume ratio: a dome encloses the most volume for the least surface area, conserving heat. A rectangle is easier to partition and expand. Neither is superior — both solve real problems. Which tradition developed which form, and why?

components.title: What Does This Shelter Say?
components.content: Choose one shelter form you have studied. Write or describe: what does this structure reveal about the people who built it? What do they value? What problems are they solving? What is the relationship between the builder and the land the structure sits on?

components.title: Igloo Engineering
components.content: The igloo is one of the most thermodynamically sophisticated shelter forms ever developed. Inuit engineers discovered that packed snow has insulating properties comparable to softwood; that a dome distributes load without internal supports; and that a cold trap entrance keeps warm air inside. Study the geometry of the corbelled dome.

components.title: Snow Properties Assessment
components.content: Learn to identify construction-grade snow by compression test and break test. Understand the relationship between snow density, crystal bonding, and block strength. Practice the hand assessment used by Inuit builders: squeeze a handful — does it hold its shape? Does it break cleanly?

components.title: Thermal Logic Across Traditions
components.content: Compare the igloo's thermal logic to the Appalachian cabin with a fireplace and the Haudenosaunee longhouse with central fires. All three use the same principle: create a warm interior volume and minimize heat loss. How does each tradition's available material shape their thermal solution?

components.title: What Does Shelter Engineering Reveal?
components.content: A Inuit elder once said that the igloo teaches patience, precision, and trust — the builder must trust the dome geometry before the last block is placed. What engineering trust is required in the Appalachian log corner notch? In the Haudenosaunee longhouse ridge pole? What does the hardest moment of each build teach?

components.title: Shelter as Cultural Text
components.content: A shelter is a document. The Haudenosaunee longhouse documents clan structure (multiple families, shared fire). The Appalachian single-pen cabin documents the frontier family economy (one room, loft, lean-to addition). The Inuit snow house documents the relationship between builder and environment (temporary, perfect for conditions, leaves no trace). Read the shelter as anthropologist.

components.title: Teaching Shelter Geometry
components.content: Demonstrate three shelter joints or techniques to a learner who has never encountered them: the saddle notch, the igloo corbel principle, and the bent-pole dome method. Adapt your explanation to the learner's background. Check for understanding at each step.

components.title: Shelter Traditions in Conversation
components.content: Modern emergency shelter design borrows from all three traditions: the dome tent from the bent-pole dome, the snow trench from Inuit cold-weather technique, the log lean-to from Appalachian woodland camping. Trace these borrowings specifically. Name the tradition, the technique, and the modern application.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Shelter
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old why different cultures built different shelters in the same climate zone. Use at least two specific examples from different traditions (Haudenosaunee, Anishinaabe, Inuit, or Appalachian). Address: how does the land shape the house? What can we learn from each tradition's solution? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: Why We Preserve
components.content: Every food preservation tradition is an answer to the same problem: the harvest is finite, but hunger is continuous. Appalachian canning converts summer abundance to winter security. Inuit meat caching extends the caribou hunt across months. Anishinaabe wild rice drying stores the fall harvest for the lean season. The technology differs; the logic is identical.

components.title: pH and Safety
components.content: Understand why low-acid foods (beans, meat, fish) require pressure canning and high-acid foods (tomatoes, berries) allow water-bath canning. The botulism threshold is pH 4.6. Above that, Clostridium botulinum can produce toxin in an anaerobic sealed jar. Know the three safe preservation principles: heat, acid, and salt/drying.

components.title: Pemmican and Jerky: A Conversation
components.content: Cree and Dene pemmican (dried meat, rendered fat, dried berries) and Appalachian jerky are both high-energy, shelf-stable trail foods. Pemmican is a complete food: fat for sustained energy, meat for protein, berries for vitamins. Jerky is simpler but less calorie-dense. Study the recipes as engineering specifications.

components.title: Fermentation Fundamentals
components.content: Document the basic biochemistry of lacto-fermentation: salt creates osmotic pressure, kills pathogens, and creates an environment where Lactobacillus thrives. Understand why sauerkraut (Appalachian German heritage) and fermented caribou (Inuit igunaq) use the same underlying biology despite vastly different climates and methods.

components.title: Preservation as Calendar
components.content: Map three preservation traditions onto the annual calendar they support. Canning season (August-September) extends to winter. Pemmican making (fall hunt) extends to spring travel. Inuit caching (seasonal hunt) extends across the dark months. How does each tradition's preservation calendar reflect the ecological rhythm of their homeland?

components.title: What Preservation Teaches
components.content: Preservation requires anticipating future need. What does it mean to work in October for the person you will be in January? How do preservation traditions encode values about community (storing for others, sharing when others run out) and relationship with the land (taking what you need, no more)?

components.title: Food Sovereignty as Heritage
components.content: Cree and Dene food sovereignty movements reclaim traditional food systems disrupted by residential schools and community relocation. Appalachian communities face food deserts in post-industrial hollows. Both are asserting the same right: the ability to feed yourself from knowledge, not just purchasing power. Food traditions are political acts.

components.title: Teaching Safe Preservation
components.content: Demonstrate safe water-bath canning technique to a learner. Cover: jar selection, headspace measurement, processing time calculation, seal verification. Cover the food safety reasoning at each step — not just the procedure, but why the procedure matters. Confirm the learner can state the pH threshold and the three safe preservation principles.

components.title: Preservation Across Traditions
components.content: Identify three specific preservation techniques shared across traditions with different implementations: drying (Appalachian jerky, Inuit fish rack, Cree pemmican), fermentation (sauerkraut, igunaq), and fat preservation (lard sealing, tallow). Name the tradition and the specific technique. Explain the underlying biology each relies on.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Food Preservation
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old why food preservation was essential to survival in heritage communities, using specific examples from at least two traditions (Cree, Inuit, Anishinaabe, or Appalachian). Address: what makes each method safe? What do these traditions share? What can someone learn from studying both? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: What Thread Carries
components.content: Thread carries more than tension. Quillwork carries Anishinaabe geometric knowledge. Inkle-woven bands carry Appalachian pattern traditions. Sinew thread carries the knowledge of how to process an animal completely. Every thread tradition encodes a relationship: with materials, with hands, with the community that passed the technique down.

components.title: Fiber Preparation Basics
components.content: Identify the three primary fiber preparation steps: cleaning (removing contaminants), processing (aligning fibers), and twisting (creating thread). Practice finger-twisting plant fiber into a two-ply cord. Understand the difference between S-twist and Z-twist and why twist direction matters when plying.

components.title: Quillwork: Technology and Art
components.content: Anishinaabe and Haudenosaunee quillwork uses porcupine quills softened in water and woven, sewn, or wrapped into geometric patterns. The patterns carry meaning specific to clan and community. The craft requires patience, precise hand tension, and design knowledge that passes within families. Study the technique without claiming access to the pattern meanings.

components.title: Loom Warp Setup
components.content: Set up a basic rigid heddle loom or backstrap loom with a 10-end warp. Calculate sett (ends per inch) for a balanced plain weave with your chosen fiber. Understand why warp tension affects weave structure. Thread the heddle. Weave a 6-inch sample in plain weave without dropped threads.

components.title: Fiber Technologies Compared
components.content: Compare two fiber technologies: the Appalachian four-shaft floor loom (industrial output, complex pattern capability) and the Haudenosaunee finger-weaving (portable, no equipment, pattern in the hands). What does each prioritize? What does each make possible? Neither is more sophisticated — they optimize for different values.

components.title: What the Hands Know
components.content: Fiber skills are embodied knowledge — the hands learn what the mind cannot fully describe. Describe a moment when your hands understood something your mind had not yet grasped. What was the skill? What changed when you felt it rather than read it? How does this experience help you understand why fiber traditions transmit through apprenticeship rather than books?

components.title: The Thread of Transmission
components.content: Fiber traditions die when the thread of transmission breaks. The revival of Haudenosaunee beadwork, Anishinaabe quillwork, and Appalachian coverlet weaving in the late 20th century required finding elders who still held the knowledge — or reconstructing from museum objects. What was lost? What was reconstructed? What is the difference between revival and continuation?

components.title: Teaching Fiber Preparation
components.content: Demonstrate sinew thread splitting or plant fiber processing to a learner. Cover the full sequence: selecting material, initial processing, splitting to working thickness, and tensioning. Explain the safety considerations (splitting tools are sharp; sinew tension can cut). Confirm the learner can independently process a working length of thread.

components.title: Fiber and Identity
components.content: Document three specific examples where fiber technique carries cultural identity: Anishinaabe clan symbols in quillwork, Haudenosaunee wampum encoding treaty relationships, Appalachian coverlet patterns naming families. Name the tradition, the technique, and the specific meaning it carries. Be precise — do not generalize across traditions.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Fiber Traditions
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old what fiber traditions reveal about the communities that created them, using specific examples from at least two traditions (Anishinaabe, Haudenosaunee, Inuit, or Appalachian). Address: what makes each tradition's approach distinctive? What do these fiber practices share across traditions? What is the role of the teacher in transmitting embodied skills? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: The Vessel as Tool and Relationship
components.content: The birchbark canoe and the qajaq were not just transportation — they were expressions of a relationship with water, a technology refined over generations, and in some traditions, living extensions of the paddler. Explore the different design philosophies: the canoe's communal capacity, the qajaq's individual precision.

components.title: Hull Form Reading
components.content: Identify the following hull features on three different watercraft (birchbark canoe, qajaq, dugout canoe): rocker (curvature of keel), sheer (curve of gunwale), beam (width), and depth. Sketch the cross-sections. Explain how rocker affects maneuverability vs. tracking.

components.title: The Qajaq as Extension of Self
components.content: In Inuit tradition, the qajaq was custom-built to the paddler's body measurements. The hull was fit to the paddler at the hip and the deck fit to the arm span. This is not metaphor — it is engineering. A qajaq fit to another paddler's dimensions is unstable. Study the relationship between builder, vessel, and water that produced this design.

components.title: Stability Analysis
components.content: Understand primary and secondary stability in watercraft design. Primary stability (resistance to initial tipping) and secondary stability (resistance to complete capsize) trade off against each other. A flat-bottomed boat has high primary, low secondary. A rounded hull has low primary, high secondary. Where does the qajaq fall? The birchbark canoe? The Northwest Coast dugout?

components.title: Water Reading Across Traditions
components.content: Compare Inuit qajaq paddling knowledge (reading sea ice, tidal patterns, weather reading from water surface) with Anishinaabe canoe route knowledge (river reading, portage selection, current use) and Northwest Coast paddling knowledge (open water navigation, tidal windows, reef reading). What knowledge does each tradition encode in its watercraft practice?

components.title: What Does It Mean to Read Water?
components.content: Water reading is a literacy — a set of skills for interpreting environmental text. What does a skilled paddler know that a novice does not? How is this knowledge transmitted? What is lost when watercraft traditions end? Consider both the practical (navigation, survival) and the cultural (relationship with water, place knowledge) dimensions of watercraft literacy.

components.title: Watercraft as Cultural Sovereignty
components.content: The revival of Haudenosaunee elm bark canoe building, Inuit qajaq construction, and Northwest Coast dugout canoe carving are all acts of cultural sovereignty. These are not museum reconstructions — they are communities reasserting their relationship with water, their knowledge systems, and their identities. Study the revival movements specifically: who led them? What knowledge was recovered? What remains?

components.title: Teaching Hull Geometry
components.content: Explain the design logic of two different watercraft hull forms to a learner who has never paddled. Cover: why the qajaq is narrow (speed and maneuverability in ice), why the birchbark canoe has rocker (turns easily in river current), why the Northwest Coast dugout is wide (open-water stability with cargo). Use diagrams. Check for understanding.

components.title: Watercraft Knowledge Systems
components.content: Compare the knowledge systems encoded in three watercraft traditions: qajaq (Inuit — body measurement, sea ice reading, rolling technique), birchbark canoe (Anishinaabe/Cree — river route knowledge, portage selection, birchbark sourcing), Northwest Coast canoe (Haida, Coast Salish — ocean navigation, tidal knowledge, carving geometry). Each vessel is a portable library of ecological knowledge.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Watercraft Traditions
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old what watercraft traditions reveal about the communities that created them, using specific examples from at least two traditions (Inuit, Anishinaabe, Cree, Haida, or Coast Salish). Address: how does the vessel reflect the water it was built for? What knowledge is encoded in the design? What is lost when these traditions are interrupted? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: The Plant as Teacher
components.content: Anishinaabe botanical knowledge distinguishes between plants that give themselves freely (wild berries, common greens), plants that require respectful harvest protocols (medicinal plants, specific berries), and plants that are not for human use. This is not superstition — it is a sophisticated resource management system that prevented overharvest. Appalachian plant knowledge carries similar practical wisdom in different language.

components.title: Safe Plant Identification Protocol
components.content: Before any plant identification: verify with three characteristics (leaf shape, stem cross-section, and one additional feature). Never rely on color alone. Never taste an unidentified plant. Confirm your identification against two independent sources. These rules apply to every plant, every time, even plants you have identified before.

components.title: Medicinal Plant Knowledge: Sovereignty and Safety
components.content: Anishinaabe, Haudenosaunee, and Cherokee medicinal plant knowledge is culturally specific — the same plant may be used differently across traditions, and the ceremonial framing matters. This knowledge is community property, not individual property. The cultural sovereignty level for most medicinal plant knowledge is Level 2-3; ceremonial plant use is Level 4. Approach this knowledge with respect.

components.title: Sustainable Harvest Protocol
components.content: Apply the one-in-seven rule: harvest no more than one plant in seven from any stand. Understand why: the remaining six plants maintain the population, provide seed, and shelter the soil ecology. Practice harvest rotation: mark the area mentally and return next season. Document the plant, the location, the date, and your harvest quantity.

components.title: Plant Knowledge Across Traditions
components.content: Identify three plants used by both Appalachian and First Nations traditions with different (but compatible) applications: yarrow (Achillea millefolium) — Anishinaabe wound treatment and Appalachian fever tea; staghorn sumac — Anishinaabe lemonade berries and Appalachian tanning agent; black walnut — Anishinaabe dye and Appalachian baking. Neither use is wrong — different applications of real botanical properties.

components.title: What Does Plant Knowledge Require?
components.content: Plant knowledge requires patience (plants reveal themselves over seasons, not days), attention (the same plant looks different in spring, summer, fall), and humility (the cost of error is real). How does this describe the nature of traditional ecological knowledge more broadly? What does learning plant knowledge teach about learning itself?

components.title: Plant Knowledge as Cultural Record
components.content: Anishinaabe, Haudenosaunee, and Cherokee plant knowledge encodes millennia of ecological observation. The names of plants in Anishinaabemowin (Ojibwe language) often describe the plant's use, ecology, or relationship to other species — the name is a data packet. Loss of language is loss of this encoded knowledge. Plant knowledge is inseparable from language.

components.title: Teaching Safe Identification
components.content: Teach the three-characteristic identification protocol to a learner in the field with a real plant. Do not simply name the plant — require the learner to identify the three characteristics themselves. When they make an error, ask questions rather than correcting directly. Confirm they can apply the protocol independently before the teaching session ends.

components.title: Plant Knowledge Sovereignty
components.content: Research the context of three plant knowledge debates: bioprospecting (pharmaceutical companies patenting plants from traditional knowledge), digital archiving (whether to digitize and publish community plant knowledge), and educational access (how much medicinal plant knowledge belongs in public educational contexts). Name the specific nations and communities involved in these debates.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Plant Knowledge
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old why plant identification requires three characteristics and why traditional plant knowledge belongs to specific nations (not to all humanity collectively). Use specific examples from Anishinaabe, Haudenosaunee, or Appalachian traditions. Address: what makes plant knowledge safe to share and what makes it appropriate to protect? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: Tools as Thinking
components.content: A tool is crystallized thought. The ulu's semicircular blade is a solution to the problem of cutting frozen hide with cold, cramped hands — the rocking motion requires less force and finger dexterity than a push-cut. The carving knife's long blade is a solution to the problem of wood grain — the push cut and pull cut each follow the grain differently. The tool encodes the problem it solves.

components.title: Tool Safety Fundamentals
components.content: Apply four cuts safely: push cut, pull cut, stop cut, pare cut. For each cut: body position (cut away from body for push cut; secure the work for pull cut), holding technique (three contact points minimum), and stopping point (know where the blade will go if it slips). Practice on softwood before working on material that matters.

components.title: The Ulu: Form Follows Function
components.content: The ulu has regional variations across Inuit, Yup'ik, Inupiat, and Greenlandic Inuit communities — each optimized for local materials and uses. The Greenlandic blade is often narrower for precision; the Yup'ik blade is often broader for hide preparation. These are not arbitrary variations but responses to specific tasks. Study the regional forms.

components.title: Edge Geometry
components.content: Understand the relationship between bevel angle, steel hardness, and cutting performance. A 15-degree bevel is razor-sharp but fragile (sushi knives, surgical blades). A 25-degree bevel is durable but less sharp (kitchen knives). A 30-degree bevel handles abuse (axes, ulus). Measure and describe the bevel angle on three tools you have access to.

components.title: Tool Traditions in Dialogue
components.content: Compare the Inuinnait (Copper Inuit) cold-hammered copper tools with Appalachian blacksmith forge-welded iron tools. Both work metal into cutting edges; neither is primitive — they are different technologies optimized for available materials. The Inuinnait had no forge and no fire-based metallurgy; their cold-hammering produced work-hardened copper edges that held.

components.title: What Does the Tool Ask of the User?
components.content: Every tool makes demands: the hand plane asks for a set stance and wrist angle; the ulu asks for a rocking motion; the draw knife asks for attention to grain direction. What does a tool's demands teach about the material it works? What does learning to use a tool correctly teach about patience, body awareness, and respect for the material?

components.title: The Life of a Tool
components.content: A well-made tool outlives its maker. Appalachian blacksmiths made tools for grandchildren. Inuit ulu handles were passed through families, with new blades ground when the old wore out. The tool carries the maker's thought and the user's adaptation. Study the concept of tool stewardship: what obligations does ownership of a well-made tool carry?

components.title: Teaching Edge Maintenance
components.content: Teach a learner to sharpen a carving knife to a working edge using a whetstone. Cover: identifying the bevel angle (keep the existing geometry), stroke direction (edge-trailing for safety and consistency), testing sharpness (paper test, hair test — not the skin test), and maintenance honing. The learner should be able to restore a working edge independently.

components.title: Tool Knowledge Transmission
components.content: Research three specific tool knowledge transmission contexts: Inuinnait cold-copper working knowledge (small community, specific region, limited documentation), Appalachian blacksmithing (regional guild and master-apprentice revival), Dene/Cree tool-making spiritual dimension (the relationship between maker and material as cultural practice). Name the nations, the tools, and the transmission methods.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Tool Traditions
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old what tool traditions reveal about the communities that created them, using specific examples from at least two traditions (Inuit, Inuinnait, Dene, Cree, or Appalachian). Address: how does a tool encode the problem it solves? What does the regional variation in ulu design teach about the relationship between tool and place? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: Music as Knowledge System
components.content: Music traditions are knowledge systems. The Appalachian ballad encodes family history and community values. Inuit katajjaq (throat singing) encodes the sound environment of the Arctic — wind, animals, ice — as a game and artistic practice. Haudenosaunee social dance songs encode ceremonial protocol and seasonal relationships. The melody is the container; the content is the culture.

components.title: Acoustic Fundamentals
components.content: Understand how a vibrating string or membrane produces sound: frequency determines pitch (faster vibration = higher pitch), amplitude determines volume (larger vibration = louder sound), and overtone content determines timbre (why a guitar and a violin playing the same note sound different). Apply this to two heritage instruments: what determines the pitch range of a dulcimer? Of a frame drum?

components.title: Katajjaq: A Publicly Shared Inuit Tradition
components.content: Katajjaq (Inuit throat singing) is a publicly shared performance tradition — the genre, the two-singer interlocking breath pattern format, and the game format are documented in Inuit cultural education workshops. The publicly shared patterns involve imitation of natural sounds: wind, animals, water. Note: specific ceremonial songs are Level 4 restricted and are not described here.

components.title: Frame Drum Construction Fundamentals
components.content: Construct a simple frame drum using a bent-steam hoop (green wood) and a hide or synthetic membrane. Understand the relationship between frame diameter and pitch (larger drum = lower pitch), membrane tension and pitch (tighter membrane = higher pitch), and frame depth and resonance (deeper frame = more bass). Note: drum construction technique is Level 1; ceremonial drum protocols are Level 3-4 and are not covered here.

components.title: Instrument Traditions Compared
components.content: Compare the banjo's African and Appalachian heritage. The banjo descends from the West African kora, ngoni, and similar gourd-and-neck instruments, brought to the Americas by enslaved African people, and adopted into Appalachian musical traditions. This history is often elided. Study the specific instrument lineage: what features of the kora appear in the early banjo? How did the instrument change in Appalachian hands?

components.title: What Music Remembers
components.content: Music preserves what written records cannot: the emotional register of historical experience. The Appalachian ballad tradition preserved stories of the Border Wars, the Atlantic crossing, and colonial settlement in living memory for generations. What did katajjaq preserve of Arctic life? What would be lost if these traditions ended? What cannot be archived?

components.title: The Line Between Public and Sacred
components.content: Every music tradition draws a line between publicly shared music and restricted ceremonial music. In Inuit tradition, katajjaq patterns are publicly shared; specific ceremonial songs belong to families and ceremonies. In First Nations traditions, social dance songs are public; medicine songs and sacred ceremony songs are restricted. The keeper understands this line and teaches it explicitly.

components.title: Teaching Acoustic Principles
components.content: Explain to a learner how a dulcimer or banjo produces its characteristic sound. Cover: string tension and pitch, fret placement and equal temperament, the relationship between scale length and fret spacing. The learner should be able to explain why two instruments of different sizes can play the same note range.

components.title: Music Sovereignty
components.content: Research three specific music sovereignty debates: the recording and distribution of sacred First Nations songs without community permission; the commercial use of Inuit throat singing patterns; the appropriation of specific Appalachian fiddle tunes by commercial country music with no attribution. Name the specific communities affected, the specific works or traditions at issue, and what resolution (if any) occurred.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Music Traditions
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old what the line between publicly shared and ceremonially restricted music means, using specific examples from at least two traditions (Inuit, Haudenosaunee, Anishinaabe, or Appalachian). Address: why does this line exist? What happens when it is crossed? How does understanding this line help a learner engage respectfully with music traditions? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

title: Who Are My Neighbors?
neighborsThread: Every community exists in relationship with other communities. The first step is mapping those relationships.

components.title: The Network of Communities
components.content: Explore how communities across traditions maintained relationships with neighbors through trade, marriage, ceremony, and mutual aid. The Appalachian barn raising, the Haudenosaunee Great Law, and the Inuit sharing economy all encode answers to the same question: how do we take care of each other and prevent conflict from becoming destruction?

components.title: Mapping Community Connections
components.content: Draw a community relationship map. Place your community (or a historical community from the pack) in the center. Add neighboring communities with lines showing the type of relationship (trade, kinship, ceremonial, territorial). Label what moves along each connection. What communities have no connection? Why?

title: Community Relations
neighborsThread: Understanding how communities maintain relationships requires understanding what those relationships are made of: reciprocity, obligation, and repair.

components.title: Reciprocity as Technology
components.content: The Potlatch (Northwest Coast First Nations — Haida, Tlingit, Kwakwaka'wakw, Coast Salish and others) redistributes wealth and encodes social relationships in public ceremony. The Appalachian corn shucking performs similar functions: labor exchange, community solidarity, status recognition. Both are social technologies for maintaining a community network across time.

components.title: Obligation Mapping
components.content: Identify three forms of community obligation present in a heritage tradition of your choice: the obligation created when you accept help (reciprocity debt), the obligation created by membership (community tax), and the obligation created by status (the wealthy host, not the wealthy hoarder). Map these in a specific community's practice.

components.title: Redistributive Systems Compared
components.content: Compare the Potlatch (wealth redistribution through ceremonial giving) with the Appalachian work party (labor redistribution through reciprocal exchange) and the Inuit sharing economy (food redistribution as survival technology). All three prevent resource concentration and strengthen community bonds. What specific mechanisms does each use to redistribute? What social norms enforce participation?

components.title: What Reciprocity Requires
components.content: Reciprocity requires tracking — who gave what, when, to whom — and a community willing to enforce the norms. What happens when someone refuses to reciprocate? How do different traditions handle defection? What does this reveal about the social infrastructure required to maintain a gift economy?

title: Conflict and Resolution
neighborsThread: Conflict resolution is the advanced skill of community relationship. It requires everything the community has built — trust, reciprocity, shared norms — and the capacity to repair when those break down.

components.title: Conflict Resolution Traditions
components.content: The Haudenosaunee condolence ceremony acknowledges grief before demanding cooperation — you cannot reason with a person in acute grief, so the ceremony clears the path. The Appalachian tradition of going to a neutral elder encodes the same wisdom: the community has designated people with the trust and skills to mediate. The Inuit tradition of moving camps when conflict cannot be resolved uses geographic separation to prevent violence.

components.title: Structured Dialogue Technique
components.content: Practice a structured dialogue format: establish relationship first (name who is in the room, acknowledge prior connection), name what happened (without blame), identify what was broken (specific, not general), discuss what repair looks like (concrete, reciprocal), close with restored relationship acknowledgment. This format appears across heritage conflict resolution traditions with different ceremonial framing.

components.title: Resolution Traditions Compared
components.content: Compare three conflict resolution traditions: the Haudenosaunee condolence ceremony (formal, ceremonially structured), the Appalachian arbitration by community elder (informal, community-endorsed authority), and the Inuit solution of physical separation (spatial, non-confrontational). What does each tradition prioritize — restoring relationship? Preventing violence? Establishing accountability? Each has a different primary goal.

components.title: What Repair Requires
components.content: Repair of broken relationships is harder than prevention. What do heritage conflict resolution traditions reveal about what human relationships require to function? What is the role of acknowledgment (naming what happened), accountability (taking responsibility), and repair (making concrete amends) in the process? Which step do modern conflict resolution approaches most often skip?

title: Bridge Builder
neighborsThread: The Keeper can explain and model conflict resolution using heritage principles. They have done the work, not just read about it.

components.title: When It Goes Wrong
components.content: Study historical examples of inter-community conflict and its resolution — the protocols for addressing raids between villages, the Appalachian tradition of making it right, the Haudenosaunee condolence ceremony. What do these protocols share? What makes them effective?

components.title: Facilitation Technique
components.content: Practice a structured dialogue format drawing from heritage conflict resolution traditions: establish relationship (acknowledge the people in the room), name what happened (without judgment), identify what was broken, discuss what repair looks like, close with restored relationship acknowledgment. This is not therapy — it is a social technology.

components.title: Heritage Principles in Modern Conflict
components.content: Map three heritage conflict resolution principles onto a contemporary situation (school, family, workplace). Name the principle, name the tradition it comes from, explain how it applies. Be specific — Haudenosaunee condolence ceremony principle: name the grief before asking for cooperation — not generic.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Bridge Building
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old how communities historically resolved conflicts without destroying relationships. Use at least two specific heritage examples. Address: what is the difference between resolving conflict and winning? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: Why Document?
components.content: The Foxfire project in Rabun Gap, Georgia began when high school students interviewed Appalachian elders and discovered that a complete technology civilization existed in living memory — one that would vanish with the elders. Haudenosaunee oral tradition and Inuit oral history face the same reality: knowledge lives in people, and people are mortal. Documentation is an act of relationship.

components.title: Interview Basics
components.content: Practice the three fundamentals of oral history interviewing: open questions (not yes/no), active listening (reflective summary, not interrogation), and consent (the interviewee controls what is shared and how it is used). Conduct a 10-minute practice interview with a family member or community member on a topic of their choosing.

components.title: OCAP and Documentation Ethics
components.content: The OCAP principles (Ownership, Control, Access, Possession) govern First Nations and Inuit knowledge documentation. They mean: the community owns its knowledge (not the researcher), controls how it is used, determines who has access, and retains physical possession of the records. These principles emerged from decades of research that extracted knowledge without benefit to communities. They apply to all documentation involving First Nations or Inuit knowledge.

components.title: Consent Protocol Implementation
components.content: Draft a consent form for an oral history interview with a heritage skills practitioner. Include: purpose statement (what the recording will be used for), scope of consent (what can be shared publicly vs. kept private), withdrawal rights (the interviewee can withdraw consent after the interview), and attribution preferences (how the person wants to be credited). Have the form reviewed by someone from the tradition before use.

components.title: Documentation Methods Compared
components.content: Compare the Foxfire oral history method (student interviewer, community elder, published compilation) with the Smithsonian Oral History methodology (professional archivist, community consent protocols, national archive) and IQ-Pilimmaksarniq (Inuit relationship-based knowledge transmission, community control). Each serves a different purpose with different consent structures. When is each appropriate?

components.title: The Ethics of Documentation
components.content: Documentation creates a record that outlives the relationship it documents. What obligations does the documentarian carry toward the person who shared knowledge? What happens when the documented person and the documentation community disagree about what should be shared? How do the OCAP principles address this?

components.title: The Heritage Book as Community Document
components.content: A Heritage Book is not a personal memoir — it is a community document. The Foxfire volumes are community documents: the students were recorders, not authors in the traditional sense. The knowledge belongs to the people who held it. The book is a vessel for that knowledge, not a claim on it. Understanding this distinction shapes every decision in Heritage Book authorship.

components.title: Heritage Book Structure
components.content: Draft the front matter for a Heritage Book project: title page (including territorial acknowledgment), table of contents with chapter descriptions, cultural sovereignty statement (required for all Heritage Books), and attribution section (naming all knowledge holders and community reviewers). Apply the attribution standards from oral history documentation.

components.title: Documentation and Sovereignty
components.content: Research the Gitxsan and Wet'suwet'en case Delgamuukw v. British Columbia (1997) — oral history was accepted as evidence of Indigenous sovereignty in Canadian courts. This changed what documentation means for First Nations communities. What documentation standards did the court establish? What obligations does this create for those who document First Nations oral history?

components.title: What Does a Heritage Book Owe?
components.content: A Heritage Book owes something to: the community it documents (accurate, respectful representation), the knowledge holders who shared (attribution, consent compliance, OCAP adherence), the readers who will use it (clear, honest framing of what is known vs. uncertain), and the future (documentation that will still be legible and accurate in 50 years). Which obligation is hardest? Why?

components.title: Documentation as Activism
components.content: The Foxfire project changed what was possible for Appalachian communities: students saw their own community as worth studying, and elders saw their knowledge as worth passing down. Haudenosaunee language documentation projects do the same: each recording session is an act of cultural continuity. Documentation is not passive — it is a choice to say this knowledge matters.

components.title: Teaching Documentation Ethics
components.content: Teach a learner the OCAP principles through a concrete scenario. Present a realistic situation: a university researcher wants to digitize a community's oral history recordings. Walk the learner through: who owns the recordings, what control the community should maintain, what access conditions are appropriate, and what possession means in the digital era. Confirm the learner can apply the principles to a new scenario independently.

components.title: Documentation Across Contexts
components.content: Compare documentation ethics in three contexts: Appalachian oral history (family and community records, generally lower sovereignty concerns), First Nations knowledge documentation (OCAP principles mandatory, community ownership), and Inuit knowledge documentation (NISR compliance, regional Inuit association oversight). Name the specific frameworks, their origins, and what they require.

components.title: Can You Teach It? — Heritage Documentation
components.content: Explain to a hypothetical 12-year-old what the OCAP principles mean and why they exist, using specific examples from First Nations or Inuit documentation history. Address: what happens to communities when researchers document their knowledge without consent or control? What would documentation look like if it followed OCAP from the beginning? Evaluators will assess: accuracy, specificity of attribution, cultural sensitivity, and whether the explanation empowers the learner.

components.title: The Seasonal Round as Knowledge System
components.content: The seasonal round is a calendar, a curriculum, and a community organizing system simultaneously. Anishinaabe seasonal rounds encode when to harvest which plants, when to fish which species, when to conduct which ceremonies, and when to move camp. Inuit seasonal rounds encode ice conditions, animal migration, weather patterns, and social gatherings. These are not primitive schedules — they are ecological monitoring systems refined over generations.

components.title: Seasonal Calendar Documentation
components.content: Create a seasonal calendar for one community in the Heritage Skills pack (choose one: Appalachian Smokies region, Anishinaabe Great Lakes, or Inuit Arctic). For each season, document: primary activities, safety considerations, and relevant ecological knowledge. Use only knowledge that is Level 1-2 (publicly or contextually shared). Note where Level 3-4 restricted content (ceremonial events) would appear without describing it.

components.title: Calendars Compared
components.content: Compare the Appalachian agricultural calendar with the Anishinaabe seasonal round and the Inuit six-season calendar (each season defined by ecological conditions, not month boundaries). What does each calendar optimize for? What ecological knowledge is encoded in each? What is lost when industrial supply chains replace seasonal awareness?

components.title: Living in Seasonal Time
components.content: Modern industrial time is abstracted from seasonal reality — the same food is available year-round; the calendar has months, not conditions. What does seasonal awareness require of a person? What does it create in a community? What is both gained and lost when a community transitions from seasonal to industrial time?
